record_identity.title: Failure-taxonomy lifecycle and multi-model deployment-cohort classification

summary: Proposal to strengthen CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 so incident classification distinguishes a defect confined to one model from shared inference, routing, release-wave, deployment-cohort, model-selection, entitlement, or common control-plane failures affecting multiple models or surfaces. The proposal also establishes an evidence-led taxonomy lifecycle so recurring VIGIL records can trigger bounded taxonomy review without treating temporal clustering as proof of a shared technical root cause.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: Status pages often report incidents by affected model or product surface, while the operative failure may sit in a shared deployment, inference, routing, entitlement, capacity, model-selection, release, or control-plane layer. Conversely, several incidents occurring close together may be technically independent. Without a deployment-cohort classification, governance records may over-count one shared failure as several model failures, collapse distinct failures into one presumed cause, misroute remediation, or obscure blast radius and recurrence. CAM therefore needs classification that preserves both observed impact and root-cause uncertainty.
evidence_confidence: corroborated classification need; technical root cause unresolved

source_records.source_title: Claude Status — repeated single-model and multi-model incidents, July 2026
source_records.model_or_algorithm: Fable 5, Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, Haiku 4.5, and multiple-model service cohorts
source_records.deployment_context: Anthropic reported several incidents on 17 July 2026 affecting different named models and surfaces, following additional incidents on 15 and 16 July involving multiple models.
source_records.source_context: The status history records separate elevated-error incidents affecting Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, Sonnet 5 and Haiku 4.5, and Fable 5, alongside adjacent incidents explicitly described as affecting multiple models. The public status material establishes repeated and cross-model impact but does not establish whether the incidents shared one technical root cause.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports a taxonomy distinction between model-specific impact, multi-model deployment-cohort impact, shared control-plane failure, and unresolved temporal clustering.

primary_artefact_access.access_method: public web review of official status history
limitations: Anthropic did not publish a technical postmortem establishing common causation for the separate incidents.
Temporal proximity and overlapping affected models are treated as classification signals, not proof of a shared root cause.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Used to establish the need for more precise incident classification while preserving root-cause uncertainty.

source_records.deployment_context: Current CAM failure-family and incident-classification taxonomy.
source_records.source_context: The taxonomy provides infrastructure-continuity, access-state, governance, state-context, classification, and related failure families, but does not presently provide a sufficiently explicit deployment-cohort or shared-control-plane classification pathway for repeated incidents spanning several model families.
source_records.relevance_note: Identifies the target instrument and the bounded classification gap addressed by the proposal.
source_records.interpretive_reliance: Used to identify existing coverage and avoid creating an unnecessary new top-level failure family.

proposal_rationale: The existing top-level failure families remain adequate. The gap is a reusable subtype and metadata pathway that separates observed impact scope from inferred technical cause. CAM should classify whether an incident is model-specific, surface-specific, deployment-cohort, shared-control-plane, cross-surface, or unresolved, while separately recording whether common causation is confirmed, suspected, disputed, or unknown.

system_context.system_type: frontier AI model services, multi-model platforms, routing layers, APIs, assistant surfaces, and deployment control planes
system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: multi-model cloud-hosted AI deployments and shared service layers
system_context.interface_surface: API, web, coding agent, model selector, routing layer, deployment wave, capacity layer, entitlement layer, and service-status surface
system_context.deployment_context: Consumer, enterprise, developer, government, research, and safety-critical workflows relying on shared multi-model infrastructure.
system_context.user_role: user, developer, administrator, operator, incident reviewer, regulator, or downstream service
system_context.affected_population: users and organisations dependent on reliable model availability and accurate incident attribution

jurisdictional_context.sector: frontier AI / cloud model services / digital infrastructure

proposal_scope.scope_summary: Amend OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 with deployment-cohort and shared-control-plane classifications, root-cause confidence metadata, and an evidence-led taxonomy review trigger tied to recurring VIGIL observations and failure modes.
automation_components: validators and indexes if controlled classification values are added
interface_components: incident dashboards and public status classification labels

recommended_controls.requirement: Classify whether the incident affected one model, several models, one surface, several surfaces, one region, one tenant cohort, or a platform-wide deployment cohort without inferring cause from scope alone.

Where evidence permits, separately classify model implementation, inference service, routing, capacity, deployment release, entitlement, model-selection, authentication, tool, client, or shared control-plane origin.
Record common causation as confirmed, probable, plausible, disputed, unknown, or not applicable. Temporal clustering must not be represented as confirmed common cause.
Define a subtype for incidents affecting multiple models or releases through a shared deployment cohort, release wave, infrastructure dependency, or control plane.
Preserve separate incidents where common cause is not established, even when they occur close together or affect overlapping products.

Trigger bounded taxonomy review when repeated VIGIL evidence reveals a structurally recurring class not adequately represented by existing subtypes, while preferring subtype extension over unnecessary new top-level families.

cam_internal.governance_layer: incident classification / deployment-cohort observability / taxonomy lifecycle
cam_internal.validator_or_automation_impact: likely if controlled subtype or metadata values are added
cam_internal.routing_note: This proposal does not create a new top-level failure family and does not assert that the cited Anthropic incidents shared one technical root cause. It strengthens classification so observed scope, suspected origin, and common-cause confidence remain distinct. PROP-0003 remains relevant only to canonical code namespace consolidation and does not duplicate this substantive taxonomy proposal.

suggested_insertion_points: Add a deployment-cohort and shared-control-plane subtype beneath Infrastructure & Continuity Failures in CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01.
Add cross-references to Classification Failures, State & Context Failures, Access-State Ambiguity, Governance Failures, and Security & Integrity Failures.
Add incident metadata axes for observed impact scope, suspected origin layer, and common-cause confidence.
Add a taxonomy-review trigger linked to repeated VIGIL evidence and amendment-ledger reconciliation.
template_changes: Consider optional VIGIL fields for incident_scope_layer, suspected_origin_layer, and common_cause_confidence.
schema_changes: Possible controlled metadata values after corpus drafting.
validator_changes: Required only if new controlled fields become mandatory.
automation_changes: Generated indexes should expose the new classification values if adopted.
interface_changes: Incident views should distinguish affected models from confirmed root cause.
migration_concerns: Existing records should not be retroactively assigned common cause without supporting evidence.
dependencies: Human review and Caelestis amendment before any VIGIL patch record is created.

migration_notes: This proposal targets prospective CAM/Caelestis corpus development and does not itself amend the taxonomy.
Temporal clustering is preserved as a signal, not converted into a technical causal claim.
